text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
URGENT RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with known germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss. Prior therapies include androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide), abiraterone/prednisone (discontinued at CRPC transition), enzalutamide (discontinued at radiographic progression), and olaparib 200 mg twice daily (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID due to Grade 3 anemia requiring packed red blood cell transfusions). Patient now presents with rising PSA (14.6 ng/mL, up from nadir of 2.1 ng/mL), new right upper quadrant discomfort, unintentional weight loss of approximately 6 lbs over 6 weeks, and newly noted hepatomegaly on physical examination. Urgent cross-sectional restaging requested by medical oncology to evaluate for visceral disease progression.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350) with scanning in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformats were reviewed. Bone windows reviewed for osseous evaluation. No prior CT chest available for direct comparison; comparison made to prior restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. There are two new soft-tissue attenuation nodules in the right lower lobe, the larger measuring approximately 0.9 cm in greatest dimension, with a second adjacent smaller nodule of 0.5 cm. A separate nodule is identified in the left upper lobe measuring approximately 0.7 cm. These nodules have rounded, well-circumscribed morphology without calcification or satellite lesions, arising in a distribution atypical for infectious or inflammatory etiology. In the clinical context of widely metastatic prostate carcinoma with known progression, these pulmonary nodules are highly suspicious for hematogenous metastatic deposits. No associated pleural effusion, consolidation, or airspace disease. No endobronchial lesions are identified.

Pleurae: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification consistent with patient's age.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: Sclerotic osseous lesions are again seen in the thoracic vertebral bodies (T10, previously identified) and in multiple posterior ribs bilaterally, consistent with known osseous metastatic disease. No new chest wall soft tissue mass. No new rib fractures identified, though the previously noted metastatic lesions at the right posterior 7th rib and left posterior 9th rib appear similar in extent.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is mildly enlarged, with the craniocaudal span measuring approximately 18.1 cm in the midclavicular line. There are multiple hypoattenuating lesions identified throughout the hepatic parenchyma that demonstrate peripheral rim enhancement on portal venous phase imaging, consistent with hypovascular metastatic deposits. The largest is located in the right hepatic lobe, segment VI/VII, measuring approximately 2.8 cm in greatest dimension. Two additional lesions are identified in the right lobe — one in segment V measuring approximately 1.7 cm, and a smaller lesion in segment VIII measuring approximately 1.1 cm. A fourth lesion is identified in the left hepatic lobe, segment III, measuring approximately 1.4 cm. These lesions were not present on prior imaging and represent new hepatic metastases. No evidence of biliary ductal dilatation. The hepatic veins and portal vein are patent. No perihepatic ascites.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is normal in appearance without cholelithiasis or wall thickening. The common bile duct measures 4 mm and is within normal limits.

Spleen: Normal in size (10.2 cm). No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreatic parenchyma is normal in attenuation and morphology. No pancreatic duct dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid collections. No focal pancreatic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: The adrenal glands are symmetric and unremarkable. No adrenal mass.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are normal in size and morphology with symmetric cortical enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No urolithiasis. No renal masses.

Stomach, Small Bowel, and Colon: Bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric stranding. No free fluid.

Peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal implants identified.

Aorta and Major Vessels: The abdominal aorta demonstrates mild diffuse atherosclerotic calcification and is normal in caliber without aneurysm. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery origins appear patent.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: There is marked enlargement of the right obturator lymph node, now measuring approximately 3.1 cm in short-axis diameter (previously 1.2 cm on prior CT at time of olaparib initiation, and 1.7 cm on most recent restaging scan approximately 3 months ago). This represents significant interval growth. A new soft-tissue density lymph node is identified in the left external iliac chain, measuring approximately 1.8 cm in short-axis diameter. This was not present on prior imaging. No new pelvic sidewall or presacral lymph nodes are identified. Mild bilateral inguinal lymph nodes are present, measuring up to 1.1 cm, which may reflect reactive changes; these will require attention on subsequent imaging.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and appears unremarkable in wall thickness. No bladder masses.

Prostate Bed: Post-biopsy changes in the prostate region. The prostate gland is not clearly delineated given prior disease involvement. No obvious large pelvic soft-tissue mass beyond the lymphadenopathy described.

Bony Pelvis and Osseous Structures: Multiple sclerotic and lytic osseous lesions are present throughout the pelvis, consistent with known metastatic prostate carcinoma. New lytic/sclerotic lesions are identified at the bilateral iliac crests, more pronounced on the right, appearing new or substantially more conspicuous compared to the prior bone scan findings. The previously identified lesion at the right sacral ala remains present. The right femoral head and neck lesion appears larger and more lytic in character compared to prior imaging, raising concern for increased disease burden at this site with potential structural compromise — the cortical integrity of the right femoral neck warrants close attention on dedicated radiographic evaluation. Lesions at L1, L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies are again noted, with the L1 and L2 lesions appearing more lytic compared to prior sclerotic change.

Sternal Lesion: A lytic lesion involving the mid-sternum, measuring approximately 1.5 cm, is again seen, consistent with previously identified metastatic focus.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW HEPATIC METASTASES: Four new hepatic lesions are identified as described, with the largest in the right hepatic lobe measuring 2.8 cm and an additional lesion in the left hepatic lobe measuring 1.4 cm. These findings are new compared to prior imaging and represent visceral progression. The liver is mildly enlarged.

2. NEW PULMONARY NODULES SUSPICIOUS FOR METASTATIC DISEASE: New nodules in the right lower lobe (dominant, 0.9 cm) and left upper lobe (0.7 cm) are identified, without a benign explanatory etiology, in a patient with known widely metastatic CRPC. These are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastatic deposits.

3. SIGNIFICANT LYMPH NODE PROGRESSION: The right obturator lymph node has grown substantially (now 3.1 cm, previously 1.2 cm at baseline olaparib initiation). A new left external iliac lymph node measuring 1.8 cm is newly identified, consistent with regional lymphatic disease spread.

4. PROGRESSIVE OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Worsening osseous disease is present with new/more conspicuous lesions at the bilateral iliac crests and more lytic change at L1-L2 vertebral bodies and the right femoral head/neck. The right femoral neck lesion warrants evaluation for structural integrity given its lytic character — dedicated plain radiograph and/or orthopedic surgery consultation may be of value for fracture risk stratification.

5. PREVIOUSLY IDENTIFIED OSSEOUS AND SOFT-TISSUE DISEASE: Known lesions at T10, multiple ribs, sacrum, and sternum are again identified without substantial change in overall extent, though these should be interpreted in the context of newly identified disease at other sites.

Overall, the findings are consistent with multi-site systemic progression on olaparib therapy, now including new visceral (hepatic and pulmonary) involvement, lymph node enlargement, and progressive osseous disease.

Electronically signed by:
Division of Abdominal and Oncologic Imaging
Department of Radiology
